# lbc-dashboard

Frontend conventions for `lbc-dashboard`.

## What this is

Localhost dashboard for `legit-biz-club` study runs. Read-only over
`legit-biz-club/.run/<ts>/<target>/<condition>/` cell sidecars.
Project-shaped UX (ensemble + artifact + per-commit history + eval
scores), separate from kbbl's session-shaped surface.

## Tech stack

- **Backend**: Bun + Hono (matches kbbl)
- **Frontend**: React 19 + Vite (matches kbbl)
- **Styling**: Tailwind CSS v4 (zero-config, `@tailwindcss/vite` plugin).
  The `prose` plugin (`@tailwindcss/typography`) is enabled for rendering
  markdown artifacts.
- **Markdown**: `react-markdown`

## Component organization

Atomic hierarchy is in the imported React standard. lbc-dashboard-specific
paths:

- `pwa/components/atoms/` — `StatusPill`, `EmptyMessage`, `TabButton`
- `pwa/components/molecules/` — `CellRow`, `EventRow`, `CommitCard`, `ScoreRow`
- `pwa/components/organisms/` — `CellList`, `CellPanel`
- `pwa/App.tsx` — top-level orchestrator (not under `components/`);
  wires hooks + state, renders organisms

Pages live in `App.tsx`; there's no routing yet (single-pane layout).
When routing arrives, page components go into `pwa/pages/`.

## Hooks + data layer

- `pwa/hooks/` — small data-fetching hooks (`useCells`, `useCellEvents`,
  `useArtifact`, etc.). One hook per resource. Each owns its `useEffect`
  lifecycle, abort handling, and refresh key.
- `pwa/lib/types.ts` — shared TypeScript types mirroring backend response
  shapes. Hand-maintained; no codegen yet. Keep in sync with `src/store.ts`.

## No write surface in v0

The dashboard reads `.run/` sidecars and serves them. Operators trigger
cells from the terminal. Adding a write side (start cell, cancel run,
edit brief) is a real architectural step that warrants a separate PR.

## What's NOT in this project (yet)

These appear in some other frontend repos' conventions but don't apply
here yet:

- **Framer Motion** — no animations needed for a dev tool.
- **React Hook Form + Zod** — no forms yet. Add when there are.
- **Next.js** — kbbl is plain Vite + React for serving a static bundle
  from Hono; lbc-dashboard mirrors that. Stay on Vite + React unless
  there's a strong reason.
- **`lib/constants.ts` for centralized copy** — there's barely any copy
  in the dashboard. Add when the volume justifies it.

## Commands

```bash
bun run --filter lbc-dashboard dev          # backend, :8765
bun run --filter lbc-dashboard dev:pwa      # frontend hot-reload, :5173 (proxies /api/*)
bun run --filter lbc-dashboard build:pwa    # static bundle to pwa/dist/
bun run --filter lbc-dashboard start        # serve built bundle from Hono on :8765
bun run --filter lbc-dashboard test         # bun test for backend
bunx tsc --noEmit                            # full-project typecheck (from oakridge root)
```

## Configuration

- `LBC_DASHBOARD_PORT` — defaults to `8765`
- `LBC_RUN_ROOT` — path to legit-biz-club's run output dir. Defaults to
  the sibling `../legit-biz-club/.run/`.

<!-- import: ../standards/core.md @ 5787c28ec1ee -->
## Two gates before building

**Stop when uncertain.** Before introducing a pattern, dependency, file, or structural
decision with no precedent in these standards or the existing codebase — stop and ask. A
wrong foundational choice is expensive to unwind; a question is cheap. This applies
especially to destructive file operations, schema and data-model decisions, new
dependencies, and changes to public interfaces.

**Data model before implementation.** No feature work begins until the domain model
exists as named types, derived from the real data source — DB schema, API contract, CLI
output, upstream types. The model is not invented to fit code as it emerges. If you
cannot point at the source the model mirrors, you do not have a model yet. Improvised
data shapes metastasize: every consumer invents its own slice, arguments proliferate,
state scatters.

## Pipelines as the default

Express logic as composable pipelines of small named transforms — each takes typed
input, returns typed output. If you cannot draw the data flow as a graph of typed nodes,
rethink the design.

- Each transform is independently testable — pure or pure-ish, reusable across flows.
- Pipelines are fractal: a node can itself be a pipeline; a cycle is a nested subgraph.
- Use a loop when it is genuinely clearer — early break, side effects, complex
  accumulation. Outside those three cases, default to the pipeline.

## Errors as values

A `Result` type (`Ok | Err`) is the connecting contract between pipeline stages. Every
fallible transform returns `Result`; the caller pattern-matches. Domain error types
carry trace context — operation name, entity id, detail — so a failure deep in a flow
can be traced to its path. `try`/`catch` lives only at IO boundaries: catch there,
convert to `Result`, hand it to the pipeline. (Concrete `Result` type: see the language
fragment.)

## Local primitives

Roll small primitives locally — `Result`, domain error types, branded ids — rather than
pull a library. They are a dozen lines, do not change, and importing them as a dependency
binds the project to someone else's release cadence for nothing in return. The language
fragments include drop-in definitions.

## Option handling

Represent absence with the language's nullable (`T | None`, `Option<T>`) — never sentinel
strings, magic numbers, or empty collections as a stand-in for "not there". When two or
more values can be absent, prefer a chain of early-return guards over nested
conditionals; flat reads better than pyramidal.

## Derived values are transforms

A derived value is a pure named function of typed input. Call it a **selector** in UI
code, a **transform** everywhere else — same idea, named for its setting. If a derivation
appears in two or more places, extract and name it. Consumers read derived values; they
never re-derive inline.

## Typing discipline

- Structured data always has a named type. Never a raw dict / untyped object /
  `Record<string, unknown>` for a known shape.
- A finite set of variants is a discriminated union — a tag plus a typed payload per
  variant — never a bare string tag beside an untyped bag.
- Domain identifiers get nominal/branded types (`UserId`, `OrderId`), not bare strings.

## Arguments

Three or more semantically related arguments → group them into one named typed object.
Long pass-through argument lists — arguments a function takes only to forward — are a
layering smell, not a formatting one: before grouping them, ask whether the intermediate
function should exist at all.

## Testing

Test behavior, not implementation — tests should survive refactors. Pure
transforms/selectors are the unit-test surface; side-effecting and IO code is tested at
its boundary. One clear assertion per test where practical; descriptive names.

## Naming

`snake_case` / `PascalCase` / `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE` per the language's norm. Descriptive
over terse — `user_count`, not `n`. Booleans read as questions — `is_active`,
`has_permission`, `should_retry`.
<!-- /import: ../standards/core.md -->

<!-- import: ../standards/typescript.md @ 5787c28ec1ee -->
## Strictness

`strict` mode on. No `any` — use `unknown` and narrow. No non-null assertions (`!`)
without a documented reason. A clean compile is a merge gate.

## Types

- A finite set of variants is a discriminated union — a literal tag plus a typed
  payload — never a string tag beside an untyped payload.
- Domain identifiers get branded types, not bare strings:

```ts
type UserId = string & { readonly __brand: 'UserId' }
```

- Never `Record<string, unknown>` (or `any`) for a shape that is known — name it.
- One definition per type. A type used on two sides lives in a shared module both
  import, never duplicated and hand-synced.

## Result

A project-local `Result<T, E>` discriminated union:

```ts
type Result<T, E> = { ok: true; value: T } | { ok: false; error: E }
```

Fallible functions return it; callers narrow on `ok`. Exceptions are for IO boundaries
only.

## Modules

Explicit named exports — avoid default exports. Absolute imports from the project root.
No re-export barrels that obscure where a symbol is defined.

## Tooling

The project's configured linter and formatter (ESLint + Prettier, or Biome) clean.
Type-check in CI, not just locally.
<!-- /import: ../standards/typescript.md -->

<!-- import: ../standards/backend.md @ 5787c28ec1ee -->
## Contracts first

Define the API or data contract as named types before writing the handler. Derive the
contract from the real schema or upstream source — do not let it emerge from the
implementation.

## IO boundaries

`try`/`catch` lives only at IO edges — network, disk, database, external processes. Catch
there, convert the failure to a `Result`, and hand it to the pipeline. Interior logic
deals in `Result`, never exceptions.

## Logging is a prerequisite

Every significant decision, state transition, and rejection is logged with enough context
to reconstruct the path that produced it. If a system is too heavy to log its decisions,
it is too heavy to run unsupervised — that is the test for whether a flow is ready, not
an afterthought.

## Persistence

Database access goes through a typed repository layer — never raw queries scattered
through handlers. Mutations are idempotent wherever the domain allows it. The persistence
schema is a defined model, not an implicit shape.

## Errors

Domain error types carry operation name, entity id, and detail, so a trace can
reconstruct where and why a failure occurred. Errors are values returned, not control
flow thrown across layers.

## Configuration and secrets

Configuration is a typed, validated object, checked at startup — fail fast on a bad
config. Secrets come from the environment; never commit them, never log them.

## Backend testing

Pure logic is unit-tested in isolation. IO and integration seams are tested at the
boundary, against real or faithfully-faked dependencies.
<!-- /import: ../standards/backend.md -->

<!-- import: ../standards/frontend.md @ 5787c28ec1ee -->
## Data model before UI

The domain model exists before any component. Types live in `types/` and mirror the real
data source — API responses, CLI output schema, DB rows — annotated with what they
mirror. One definition per type; a type needed on two sides lives in a shared module both
import, never defined twice. No component is written before the shape of the data it
consumes is a named type.

## Component composition

Components are small and single-responsibility. Keep presentational components (props in,
markup out) separate from components that own state or fetch data. Composition over
configuration — many small components over one component with many flags.

## Derived values

Pure derivation functions live in `lib/selectors.ts` — they are called selectors in
UI code, transforms everywhere else. A
derivation used in two or more places is extracted and named. Components never derive in
the render body — they read selector output. Selectors are pure and are unit-tested as
library code, with no component rendering.

## State layering

- **Local** — ephemeral, single-component state.
- **Shared** — state crossing components; lives in a store. Cross-component state is
  normal in any non-trivial UI; its absence usually means isolated pages or
  prop-drilling, both UX failures.
- **Server** — remote data with cache / refetch / mutation needs; managed by a query
  layer, not hand-rolled in effects.

Specific libraries: see the framework fragment.

## Styling

One styling system, applied through utility classes — no CSS modules, no
styled-components, no inline style objects. Use the system's responsive and theme
utilities rather than ad-hoc values.

## Anti-patterns

- Deriving in the render body — extract a selector.
- Prop-drilling domain state through three or more layers — use shared state.
- Typing a known shape as `any` or an untyped bag — name the type; if it has variants,
  make it a discriminated union.
- Building UI before the data model exists — the expensive one.
<!-- /import: ../standards/frontend.md -->

<!-- import: ../standards/react.md @ 5787c28ec1ee -->
## Atomic design

Three tiers; the boundary is a hard rule, not a judgment call:

- **Atom** — renders a single visual element. No state, no data fetching, no logic.
- **Molecule** — composes two or more atoms. Presentational only; may hold trivial view
  state (a hover, an open/closed toggle) but never owns domain state and never fetches.
- **Organism** — owns state or fetches data, or both. Composes atoms and molecules into a
  feature section. Organisms are what pages render.

Operational test, in order: owns state or fetches → organism; only composes atoms →
molecule; single element → atom. "Might need state later" does not make a molecule —
promote it when that day comes. Framework-coupled node types (e.g. React Flow nodes) sit
outside this hierarchy; document them per project.

## State

- Shared / cross-component, or persisting beyond one feature → a **Zustand** store,
  consumed via slice selectors (`useStore(s => s.field)`) so components re-render only on
  the slices they use.
- Component-local, ephemeral → `useState`.
- Server data needing cache / refetch / mutation → **TanStack Query**; plain fetch in a
  hook when none of those are needed.

## Selectors

Pure derivation functions in `lib/selectors.ts`, unit-tested as library code. `useMemo`
only when reference stability matters — a memoized child, an expensive computation — not
as a reflex.

## Components

Functional `.tsx` with a typed props `interface`; destructure props in the signature.
Callback props use the `on*` prefix (`onClose`, `onSelect`). Group three or more related
props that travel together into a named type.

## Testing

Tests cover `lib/` logic only — transforms, selectors, query shaping. No component-render
tests: they churn with markup and mostly re-assert what the types already guarantee.
`lib/` is the stable, behavior-bearing surface.
<!-- /import: ../standards/react.md -->
